assumptions: The declared intragroup dividend is denominated in a foreign currency and has created an intragroup receivable/payable that is a monetary item.
The analysis is for consolidated financial statements under IFRS 9, not for separate financial statements.
The facts do not state whether the foreign exchange effects survive consolidation or affect consolidated profit or loss, so conditional IFRS tests are highlighted.

approaches.label: Hedge of intragroup monetary receivable in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 expressly permits the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item in consolidated financial statements if the resulting FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation. A declared dividend receivable can fit this route if it is a monetary receivable/payable and the hedge is properly designated.
conditions: The dividend has created an intragroup monetary receivable/payable
The counterparties have different functional currencies
The FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
The hedged FX risk is separately identifiable and reliably measurable
The IFRS 9 designation, documentation and effectiveness requirements are met
approaches.practical_implication: If used, the hedge should be documented against the residual consolidated FX exposure, not the eliminated intragroup balance as such.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows foreign currency risk on intragroup monetary items in consolidation when FX differences are not fully eliminated.
Paragraphs 6.3.1, 6.3.7 and 6.4.1 require an eligible hedged item/risk plus formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of forecast intragroup dividend
approaches.reasoning: The forecast intragroup transaction exception applies only to highly probable transactions whose FX risk will affect consolidated profit or loss. A dividend that has already created a receivable is no longer merely forecast, and the context gives no basis that its FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss.
conditions: The transaction would need to remain highly probable and not yet recognised
It would need to be denominated in a currency other than the functional currency of the transacting entity
Its foreign currency risk would need to affect consolidated profit or loss
approaches.practical_implication: Do not base the hedge documentation on the forecast-transaction model for the stated fact pattern.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 6.3.6 and B6.3.5 allow forecast intragroup FX hedges only when the FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss.
Paragraphs 6.3.4 and 6.5.2(b) frame this route as a cash flow hedge of a highly probable forecast transaction.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge of a foreign operation
approaches.reasoning: The net investment hedge model is reserved for foreign currency risk on all or part of a foreign operation's net assets, not an individual intragroup dividend receivable. IFRIC 16 treats this as a separate hedge accounting model with its own designation boundaries.
conditions: The hedged item would need to be an amount of net assets of a foreign operation
approaches.practical_implication: Use this model only if the documentation is truly tied to the foreign operation's net investment rather than to the dividend claim.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.5.2 identifies a hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation as a distinct type of hedge relationship.
Paragraphs 10-14 limit the model to net investment FX risk and define the hedged item as all or part of the foreign operation's net assets.
